tagged_story_for_tts:
Cities as heat sponges.<break time=800/> Concrete that won’t cool.<break time=800/>

The term urban heat island describes how cities trap heat like a car left in the sun—except the "windows" are asphalt, steel, and glass.<break time=800/> Pavement absorbs 95% of sunlight, reradiating it as heat long after sunset.<break time=800/> Trees, which cool air through evaporation, are replaced by buildings that block breezes like a wall of dominoes.<break time=800/>

At night, rural areas drop 10°F (5.5°C) faster than downtowns.<break time=800/> Tokyo’s center stays 9°F (5°C) hotter than its outskirts;<break time=200/> Phoenix’s asphalt hits 160°F (71°C)—enough to fry an egg.<break time=800/> Air conditioners dump waste heat outside, turning sidewalks into griddles.<break time=800/> The fix? Paint roofs white (Los Angeles cooled 2°F this way), plant "cool pavements" with light-colored stones, or mimic termite mounds—whose vents regulate temperature passively.<break time=800/>

Three related subjects are nighttime cooling tricks, heat-resistant building materials, and how desert animals survive.<break time=800/>
clean_story:
Cities as heat sponges. Concrete that won’t cool.

The term urban heat island describes how cities trap heat like a car left in the sun—except the "windows" are asphalt, steel, and glass. Pavement absorbs 95% of sunlight, reradiating it as heat long after sunset. Trees, which cool air through evaporation, are replaced by buildings that block breezes like a wall of dominoes.

At night, rural areas drop 10°F (5.5°C) faster than downtowns. Tokyo’s center stays 9°F (5°C) hotter than its outskirts; Phoenix’s asphalt hits 160°F (71°C)—enough to fry an egg. Air conditioners dump waste heat outside, turning sidewalks into griddles. The fix? Paint roofs white (Los Angeles cooled 2°F this way), plant "cool pavements" with light-colored stones, or mimic termite mounds—whose vents regulate temperature passively.

Three related subjects are nighttime cooling tricks, heat-resistant building materials, and how desert animals survive.
